data.title: FEPI: High Tech, High Income, Both Good Things For 2026
data.description:
The REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI) has been on my radar for a while, since I said that ignoring this fund was a mistake a year ago. I've held my buy rating on the fund since then, including my most recent coverage from back in May, when I dissected the strategy.

The fund's strategy is fairly straightforward; it is a covered call fund. Its mandate is to own a basket of stocks and sell options against them for income. The trade-off for immediate, high income is a capped upside on the stocks being sold against. For many income investors, this is a worthwhile trade.

FEPI's basket of stocks is what makes it unique. It holds the "Magnificent 15" as I've called them in the past. This is a group of stocks, which includes the famous Magnificent 7, and others that are high volatility and fit a broad "innovation" theme.

High volatility usually means high income, but also a steeper lag of the uncovered indexes. This is a trade-off income oriented investors must consider and understand here. There is no perfect benchmark for FEPI, like a fund that holds the same stocks but uncovered, so I'll use the Nasdaq 100 and the broad technology sectors as comparison here.

That lagging is a feature, because unlike these other funds with dividend rates below 1%, FEPI distributes 25% p.a.

FEPI Overview

Price $46.50
Dividend TTM $11.71
Distribution Rate 25.20%
RoC % ~99%
Dividend Freq Monthly
AUM $588.94M
ER 0.65%

The fund has a mandate that allows its managers to be flexible with their stock inclusions, and so while we have the Magnificent 7, there are other firms that fit the broader theme of "innovation" included too, like AppLovin (APP), Intel (INTC), etc.

These firms are all held at equal weights, so any higher or lower weight is a manifestation of the stock's out- or underperformance since the last quarterly rebalance.

Geographically, all the holdings are based in the US. There is no specific mandate I could find that locks the holdings to the US, but there is a natural tendency for these kinds of funds to concentrate entirely on domestic stocks.

The same goes for the market cap, where there is no rule that I could find that says they only have to include large cap stocks, but that's all that's in the fund. It makes sense to bet on more liquid stocks like large and mega caps; it may be difficult to sell options against many mid- and small cap stocks.

Sector wise, there is a high concentration in tech and communications services, which makes sense to me in fitting with the "innovation" theme. Note that the retail and durables allocations are to Tesla (TSLA) and Amazon (AMZN). The latter is misleading because even though it is seen as a retailer, most of its revenue comes from cloud services.

Outside of April, the fund hasn't had any negative outflow months in the last year, with a net 205M inflows in the last year. This is bullish for the fund's continuing operations, and shows that the fund is still growing its assets regularly.

FEPI Uses An Options Strategy I Prefer

One of the differences between FEPI and other tech-oriented covered call funds -- other than the underlying holdings, which are unique to FEPI -- is the options strategy. Instead of selling options against an index, like the value-oriented JEPI ETF does with the S&P 500, FEPI allows its managers to actively manage options on each individual holding.

This leads us to a holdings page that looks like this, where positions are individually taken against each holding every month. These are routinely rolled each month, and this flexibility allows for managers to stake out a more directional bias on each holding.

While it makes it very difficult to gauge their success, or their strategy (it is a black box as far as I can tell), I believe that this is an advantageous way to structure a covered call fund. It relies more heavily on manager skill than a passive structure, but also allows for more flexibility. If investors trust the managers, this structure is advantageous and preferable to a passive index.

We can see a breakdown of FEPI's performance against other tech-oriented covered call funds, although these two are based on the Nasdaq 100, because FEPI has no true non-covered call counterparts. JEPQ's options are actively managed, but they stick to the index, and QYLD is passively managed throughout.

FEPI Dividends & Taxes

Because FEPI has this active component to its options management strategy, it also means that the income is more variable, another trade-off to consider. While its income is high, nobody can deny a 25% distribution rate, that income doesn't always come in even amounts.

While investors may be able to consider that most dividends in the last year were paid inside the $1.00-0.90 range, outliers do happen, and no certain level is guaranteed.

From a tax perspective, FEPI is tax-efficient and could be considered as a holding for taxable accounts. This is because the return from options contracts within the ETF structure is considered capital gains and losses. This means that options income, when passed to shareholders, is considered return of capital ("RoC").

RoC is considered advantageous by many income investors because it counts against your cost basis. This means that almost all income passed from FEPI to shareholders does not create taxable events. Instead, investors create the taxable event when they sell the shares. This allows for investors to have more control over when they pay taxes, and for those taxes to be paid at the (most likely) lower capital gains tax rate compared to the income tax rate.

If your cost basis hits zero, all future distributions will be counted as taxable in the year they are earned but qualify as long-term capital gains, according to this IRS publication.

FEPI estimates its RoC, which is currently at 100% for the year, according to the latest 19a-1 forms that were published.

This distinction does not necessarily affect investors in tax-sheltered accounts, so keep that in mind.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am still a fan of FEPI moving forward; as a bull going into 2026, I see tech exposure as still likely to outperform. FEPI offers a unique strategy that is tilted toward tech, and income-oriented investors could benefit from increasing their exposure to big tech, which I see as continuing to be the biggest winners of ongoing market forces.

This has reinforced my buy rating for FEPI, which I am maintaining here as we go into next year. Income investors wanting innovation exposure should still consider FEPI as a satellite holding in an income portfolio.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: MPLX: The Best MLP For Income
data.description:
Introduction

Following my recent catch-up phase with midstream companies, a sector that I really enjoy investing in due to its basic business model, overall undervaluation, and great yield, I want to revisit MPLX, a Strong Buy of mine. Since my last analysis in mid-July, the company has been one of the rare outperformers compared to the S&P500, with its total return of 13.84%.

MPLX (NYSE: MPLX) is the midstream arm of Marathon Petroleum and has been highly active in the past couple of months with its earnings release as well as multiple deals that have strengthened the company’s position in the Permian. I want to see if MPLX is worth investing in now or if it is better to buy other competitors.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first go through the latest earnings for MPX, which was a strong double beat with Revenue coming in at $3.62B, a massive 21.9% Y/Y increase and a beat of around $460MM. On the bottom line, Q3 GAAP EPU came in at $1.52, a massive beat of 44 cents, though for MLPs, it is better to have a look at EBITDA, that came in at $1.77B, up 3% Y/Y. MPLX has clearly bucked the MLP trend with relatively mixed earnings despite record volumes. DCF stood at $1.5B, easily funding the $1.1B of return capital. The main headline came in from the distribution side with MPLX realizing a second consecutive annual hike of 12.5%. That is simply massive considering the current dynamics the industry is facing, and thus pushes the quarterly distribution to $1.0765. Furthermore, MPLX has stated that they are targeting a 12.5% annual distribution growth target through 2027, which would result in an annualized distribution of $5.45, giving a yield on cost of around 9.7%.

Going by segments, L&S has recorded Adj. EBITDA of $1.14B thanks to higher tariff rates and higher volumes through their crude systems, and this should continue with the FID on the Eiger Express pipeline that will move around 2.5Bcf/d out of the Permian. On the G&P side, Adj. EBITDA came in at $629MM thanks to the high volumes in the Marcellus and Utica Basins. This has also supported management stating that growth in 2026 will exceed the 2025 levels, with new assets being placed in service as well as mid-single-digit adj. EBITDA growth outlook for 2025 and further down the road.

On the deal side, MPLX rapidly completed the $2.4B deal for Northwind Midstream that was initially announced in late July. This will be a specialized gathering and treating system in the Permian, and I have to say that it is rather smart. As the Permian begins to age, the region will need to be drilled deeper and thus encounter more and more sour gas. This requires special handling that many pipelines can not provide. MPLX will triple the system’s capacity to around 440 MMcf/d by late 2026. Furthermore, the system is currently highly contracted by producers such as Coterra and Matador. Northwind also owns rare permits for AGI wells in order to dispose of the deadly sulfur, thus directly giving MPLX a specialized MOAT in this category. I believe that this is a very smart deal for MPLX that came in at a rather low cost for the future potential. Producers will require more and more to pay premium rates for sour gas treatments, and MPLX will be right there to get them.

On the flip side, MPLX divested its Rockies operations as it was seeing flat to declining activity, as it was highly sensitive to price drops. The kind of assets meant that MPLX had to spend maintenance capital just to keep EBITDA flat. The move to sell it to Harvest Midstream was the right one in my opinion, as the company will directly receive $1B in cash that can be deployed to pay back debt on the Northwind Midstream deal or increase distribution to unitholders.

MPLX is also following in the trends of its competitors by signing strategic deals such as the one with MARA Holdings, in which MPLX is to facilitate the supply of nat gas to power generation facilities and data centers in West Texas. This will basically act as a tolling agreement where MPLX supplies and MARA builds the infrastructure that gives back cheap and reliable power to MPLX’s own operations, with plans to scale up to 1.5 Gigawatts.

Valuation

MPLX is currently on the pricier side of midstream companies with its current EV/EBITDA of 13.58, though I believe that it is mainly from the company’s good momentum, while the industry is on a standby mode and also having some of the best profitability metrics around. Right now, you’re paying for a relative growth premium compared to close MLPs such as Energy Transfer and Enterprise Products Partners that are having a slower and steadier strategy. This is highlighted by the fact that EV/EBITDA is pricier than over the past 5 years despite a huge increase in EBITDA. The 12.5% increase is rather rare and unexpected, and that’s why many have been piling into the company’s unit, which has led to a short rally. This means that MPLX is currently the best MLP on the block in terms of distribution growth with its yield of 7.15%, which is justifiable to pay a higher price now to lock in the yield that essentially doubles in 6 years at this current pace. The ease of FCF generation means that MPLX also has a fair FCF yield of 8.35%.

I believe that right now the growth prospects are the strongest for MPLX, especially with its push into Sour Gas, which has been a sort of “ha ha” moment. I’ve already talked in length about the distribution growth, but I believe that the company will be able to incrementally increase EBITDA thanks to projects mainly in natural gas and NGL coming online, as well as inflation-adjusted escalators kicking in.

As mentioned, MPLX is renowned for its profitability levels in the industry, mainly thanks to the high utilization rates that the company benefits from being associated with MPC, which is highly dependent on MPLX to move its products. MPLX also has a presence all over the value chain and can capture fees from the exact same molecule multiple times. The minimum volume commitments also act as a safety net, as even if MPC does not ship oil or gas, it will still have to pay fees to MPLX. With its scale, MPLX has been able to improve its overall margins over the past 10 years, with EBITDA margin reaching 51.4%, up from 46.6% back in 2015. FCF margin has, however, drastically improved from 8.7% back in 2015 to now 40.3% thanks to a CapEx diet that saw the 2020-2025 period focusing on discipline as well as the double dip profit mode that arose from MPLX’s scale.

Risks

The big risk in general for companies like MPLX would be its single customer concentration, with MPC accounting for over 50% of the company’s revenue. If there were to be an incident at an MPC refinery, or let’s say the US invades Venezuela, refineries would not be able to import heavy crude from the country, and would reduce MPLX’s overall throughput volumes.

The company’s valuation now also priced in the gigantic growth in distribution, and if the company were to decrease it, let’s say to an already strong level of 6% in 2026, the unit price would drastically drop, as many income investors would look at other better growth around the market.

Conclusion

I believe that even more now than before that MPLX is a great buy for your income-focused portfolio. It has made smart deals that should further push the company’s profitability levels to new heights despite having some of the best. The distribution hikes are a major tailwind, and the company has a strong enough distribution coverage to continue on this fast trend. I keep my strong buy rating on MPLX.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seagate: Why The HAMR Era Isn't Priced In Yet
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I first initiated coverage on Seagate Technology Holdings (NASDAQ:STX), my confidence stemmed from STX's strong execution, secular tailwinds in data storage, and the rollout of HAMR technology. Yet since then, the stock has surged by 137% as of writing this article. In Q1 2026 earnings results, Seagate delivered 21% y/y revenue growth amid booming demand from cloud and AI use cases. The company is at the center of a data storage boom driven by hyperscale cloud expansion and gen AI. I believe STX leadership in HAMR technology will enable much higher drive capacities. This will in turn allow the company to reap outsized gains in the years to come. And inasmuch as the stock has more than doubled since coverage, the bottom line is that I see STX's best days are still ahead. For this reason, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

I would like to focus more on HAMR, which I believe is key for STX to capitalize on secular tailwinds around the data storage space.

The HAMR Tech Ramp-Up

In my previous article, I dwelled at length on Seagate's next-gen HAMR tech. And it was for a good reason. HAMR is a game changer for drive capacity. After years of development, STX has begun its phased rollout of HAMR-based drives. Customers are eager to deploy them. The company already started volume shipments of a 32 TB HAMR to at least one major cloud provider [which is largely believed to be Google (GOOG)(GOOGL)]. According to the CEO, Dave Mosley,

"We continue to execute our areal density leading HAMR product qualification and ramp plans... positioning Seagate to address customer growth for mass storage across the cloud and at the edge...

Three of the top eight hyperscalers have qualified Seagate's HAMR drives, and early shipments are at exabyte scale. This gives Seagate a head start in delivering the highest capacity disks on the market. By mid-2026, I believe the company will have all its cloud customers to be qualified on HAMR drives. This means that by late 2026, HAMR drives will account for the majority of its exabytes shipped. And this is such a major inflection point.

The market opportunity for HAMR is quite enormous. According to Horizon Technology, drive capacity shipments will grow at 19% CAGR through 2027. And this will be driven by data center demand. On the other side, International Data Corp. [IDC] and S&P Global also expect that global storage capacity is anticipated to grow by 25% annually over the next five years. This implies that by 2027, annual HDD shipments could exceed 2 zettabytes. And if HAMR-based models comprise over half of those shipments by that time, then it means that we are talking about hundreds of exabytes of HAMR drives shipping per year. Now, in dollar terms, even a few hundred exabytes at modest pricing would translate to several billion dollars in revenue. I would say that Seagate, as a first mover in HAMR and a dominant supplier, is well positioned to capture a large chunk of this transition. For context, in just the first quarter of 2025, the HDD industry shipped 361 exabytes of capacity. And this yields a run rate of 1.4 zettabytes per year. And according to Tom Coughlin's Digital Storage Technology Newsletter, there will be a steep rise in HDD exabyte shipments through 2030 as demand for low-cost storage explodes, as shown below. This ideally means that HAMR is the tech that will unlock that growth by continuously driving up capacity per drive.

It is also important to consider AI-related storage demand in more detail. Training and deploying advanced AI models requires massive datasets and numerous model checkpoints to be stored. And these are tasks that are well suited for high-capacity HDDs. Perhaps a better way to explain this is using Meta's (META) planned Prometheus AI supercomputer. It is estimated to need on the order of 10 to 20 exabytes of storage for training data and model outputs. Yet META expects to build multiple such clusters, indicating tens of exabytes of new storage demand from just one organization's AI initiatives. Better still, gen AI content is exploding. According to Kerrisdale Capital, 15 billion images were created by AI in just 18 months in 2023, and by 2028, AI-driven image or video creation is projected to be 167x larger than in 2023.

So what does this mean?

It means that all these data needs to reside somewhere. And HDDs excel at exactly this type of large-scale and infrequently accessed cold storage. Even high-performance AI systems utilize HDD tiers for cost efficiency. As an example, META's AI research cluster uses HDD as the bulk data tier beneath faster flash caches. At the end of the day, these trends provide a strong third-party validation that Seagate's core markets will definitely see sustained and hefty demand for the foreseeable future.

Now that HDDs are expected to take over, what exactly happens to SSDs?

Well, the current reality is that HDDs will remain dramatically cheaper on a per terabyte basis, and HAMR ensures they will stay the lowest-cost bulk storage solution for a long time. As of today, HDDs are roughly 6X cheaper per TB than flash-based SSDs. In fact, disk drives will retain a 6:1 $/TB cost advantage over SSDs through at least 2027. And this gap is unlikely to close soon because NAND flash costs are no longer falling at the pace they once did. Meanwhile, I believe that HAMR is re-accelerating HDD density gains after years of slower progress. Traditional Perpendicular Magnetic Recording [PMR] drives were approaching a real density limit. And the beauty of HAMR is that it is breaking those limits by using laser heating to write to smaller magnetic grains. For instance, STX's current HAMR drives deliver 1 TB/in², which equates to more than 3 TB per platter. Already, the company has demonstrated 6.5 TB per platter in the lab and sees a path to 10 TB per platter by 2028. This means that there is a possibility of 100 TB drives in the early 2030s and even 150 TB drives with further tech advances. A competitor like Western Digital (WDC), which I also covered, has outlined a similar roadmap of growing to 100 TB per platter by 2030. The advantage that STX has is that WDC is years behind in development. Therefore, the key point is that no fundamental tech wall is in sight yet for HDD capacity. And this is what makes STX a win.

SSDs will certainly keep getting larger and cheaper in absolute terms, but scaling flash to match zettabytes of demand will be extremely costly. According to IDC data, by 2027 the world will need roughly 3686 exabytes of combined storage capacity per year, with HDDs expected to supply about 2723 EB and NAND flash around 963 EB. Now, if we tried to make up that 2.7 ZB solely with flash memory, then the estimate would be about $206 billion. And this is quite not feasible given the timeframe. In simple terms, the economics and sheer volume requirements mandate a large role for HDDs. HDD manufacturing is about 9x more capital efficient than SSD manufacturing. And this is why even by 2027, high-capacity drives will be indispensable for bulk data storage. And this is why STX's execution in HAMR positions it as a long-term winner in the storage ecosystem. Because it also offers customers a much lower $/TB and sufficient performance for large-scale data while also enjoying improved economics.

Now that we have deeply looked at HAMR and how it positions Seagate in the future, let's dig into the valuation.

Valuation

First, I have elaborated in detail how HAMR positions STX into the future. And this can be further stressed using its growth grade. I note that the FWD revenue growth is 24.42%, while the sector is expected to grow by a modest 7.9%, as shown below. When I first covered the stock, the FWD revenue growth stood at about 11%. This improvement from 11% to 24% is partly due to the expected margin boost from HAMR. As I said earlier, the new drives command premium pricing and will definitely lift STX's gross margins. Already, early evidence from the Q1 2026 earnings suggests that gross margins could expand towards 40% as HAMR shipments scale. And this clearly shows that STX is entering a cycle of accelerating earnings where revenue is growing double digits and margins are inflecting upward.

When I look at the valuation numbers, I note that Seagate's stock has not fully caught up to its improved prospects. The FWD non-GAAP PEG ratio is at 0.99x, which is below the sector median of 1.77x. This suggests that STX is 43% discounted relative to the sector.

When I look at the FWD GAAP P/E, the company trades at 28.82x, while the sector median stands at 32.02x. This suggests that the company is trading at a discount relative to the sector median. Seagate's FWD GAAP P/E multiple is slightly above its direct HDD competitor, Western Digital (WDC). It is also higher than Micron's (MU) inasmuch as MU's low P/E reflects the volatile cyclical nature of the memory business.

On an FWD EV/EBITDA basis, still Seagate's multiple [19X] is a bit higher than WDC [15X] and MU [8X]. And this is partly because Seagate's EBITDA was coming off a cyclical trough. FWD EBITDA multiples would be lower as margins expand.

At the end of the day, when I look at STX's valuation numbers, I believe its valuation sits somewhere in the middle of the pack. And I believe this is a positive sign given its strong growth outlook. It definitely commands a justifiable premium to WDC given that it is ahead on HAMR. Compared to the broader sector, STX is quite cheap on a PEG basis. I believe that if STX executes on its HAMR ramp and delivers on the expected earnings growth, then definitely there is room for an upward re-rating.

Seagate's Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for Seagate right now is the possibility of an AI or cloud capex slowdown. The company is riding a wave of heavy investments by hyperscalers in data center infrastructure. Especially for AI training clusters and expanded cloud storage. If, for any reason, that capex cycle pauses and decelerates, then the demand for nearline HDDs could quickly soften. And as I noted before, hyperscalers are investing aggressively in AI and cloud capacity, but they are also periodically reassessing spending. Therefore, a pullback in orders from one or more big customers would be detrimental to STX's near-term growth.

Final Thoughts

Seagate stock may have doubled since my initial coverage, but I believe there is still significant upside ahead. The company is on a major tech transition that should drive higher capacities, higher margins, and a new earnings upcycle. Yet, the valuation numbers continue to speak volumes, especially the FWD Non-GAAP PEG ratio. I believe that as HAMR ramp continues through 2026, the company is positioned to expand its margins, which will eventually boost its growth prospects. I still believe STX deserves a valuation above the traditional HDD peers.

data.title: Quarterly Outlook: Cross-Country Divergences
data.description:
Global Macro Outlook: Cross-Country Divergences

By Paul Mielczarski

Despite significant macroeconomic and trade policy shocks, 2025 proved a remarkably quiet year for developed market bond yields. The year-to-date high-low yield range of approximately 36 basis points was exceptionally narrow compared to the average annual range of around 80 basis points for the JP Morgan Global Bond Index over the previous decade. Heading into 2026, we expect developed market yields to remain broadly range-bound. On the one hand, there are several factors that point toward the possibility of acceleration of global growth: reduced tariff drag, supportive fiscal policies in major economies, and favorable financial conditions. However, ongoing weakness in employment growth creates meaningful downside risks and limits the scope for a sustained move higher in yields.

Within this sideways aggregate picture, we see significant cross-country divergences. In the UK, we see scope for further yield declines as the Bank of England responds to the combination of deteriorating labor market conditions, falling inflation, and fiscal drag. Conversely, eurozone yields are likely to drift higher as the economy benefits from the lagged impact of substantial European Central Bank easing and the large multi-year German fiscal stimulus package. This UK-eurozone spread compression remains one of our highest conviction views.

In the currency markets, we expect further US dollar (USD) weakness in 2026. The dollar remains overvalued against most G10 and emerging market currencies. We anticipate ongoing convergence in relative growth rates after a prolonged period of US exceptionalism. Global investors remain structurally overweight USD assets, with both economic and geopolitical incentives to reduce these exposures over time. Interest rate differentials are moving against the dollar. Developed market central banks outside the US are more likely to tighten policy in the future while the Federal Reserve (Fed) remains in a dovish mode, focusing on labor market downside risks. The change in Fed leadership next year should impart an even more accommodative bias.

The macro backdrop for emerging market (EM) local currency bonds remains supportive. EM bonds should continue to benefit from elevated real yields, attractive currency valuations, the absence of major macroeconomic imbalances, and limited foreign ownership levels. In the medium term, EM local currency assets are well positioned to benefit from a softening dollar environment and growing appetite among global investors to reduce US asset concentration.

Developed Markets: Relative Value Remains Key

By Jack P. McIntyre, CFA

We are not going into early 2026 with a pound-the-table level of conviction regarding developed market (DM) bonds. One reason is the US is playing economic data catch-up, and the quality of that data is a little suspect. Add in a combination of a still slightly softening labor market and consumption-influenced fiscal stimulus on the immediate horizon, and these factors lead me to stick with my multi-year theme of earning the coupon in fixed income. Or, said another way, yield (income) over duration (capital gains) should be the driver of total return in DM fixed income.

Heading into year end, US 10-year Treasury yields are hovering close to 4%. This level, absent a notch lower in economic growth, should be closer to the bottom of the yield range. If the labor market starts printing weaker numbers, which is not our base case, a probe into the 3.50% to 3.80% range could be in the offing. However, when you have a central bank easing monetary policy married with a government still running 6% deficits in a non-recessionary environment, it is hard to imagine a recession is likely. We would give it extremely low odds. If the US economy gains traction in the first quarter, which we see as a possibility given the onslaught of tax refunds set to hit, a move back toward 4.50% seems plausible. Above that level, the Trump administration would have to move toward less fiscal responsibility for the bond vigilantes to reappear in force, which, again, is not our base case. We are coming into the new year neutral duration and not positioned for a big shift or twist in the yield curve.

It is also difficult to get too bullish on lower long-end yields when you have a Federal Reserve (Fed) cutting policy rates at a time when inflation continues to run above the target rate. Granted, future rate cuts should not be a sure thing in this environment, but we are going to be dealing with a new Fed chair in 2026. Will it be someone closely tied to the current administration? It feels like the next decade could be something akin to the 1970s, when Arthur Burns and Bill Miller ran the Fed. Active bond management will do well if we get a repeat of that world.

Away from the US, the DM bond world is going to be more influenced by relative value trades. I am open to the idea that DM bond markets lose some of their correlations as idiosyncratic, or bottom-up, influences become more important than the top-down, global macro forces. We still expect the gilt market to outperform broader DM Europe, as the UK has started the process of fiscal retrenchment when other European governments are still favoring fiscal expansion.

For us to get excited about owning Japanese government bonds (JGBs) in 2026, the Bank of Japan must be fully committed to tightening policy rates to a point that breaks the back of inflation in Japan. We are not there yet. JGB yields need to continue to grind higher, and inflation, which may have peaked, needs to head lower. Again, we are not there yet.

Too much debt while the price of that debt increases is a toxic mix. The bond market will discriminate via the term premia lever in 2026, which supports our core view that relative value trades will be the way to make outsized returns in the DM bond space in 2026.

Investment Grade: A Bond-Picker’s Market

By Brian L. Kloss, JD, CPA

As we move toward 2026, investment grade (IG) corporate credit increasingly resembles a “bond-picker’s market.” The broad beta trade that rewarded investors over the past two years has largely run its course; spreads have compressed and dispersion has widened.

Valuations in IG credit remain far from compelling. After years of yield-seeking flows—particularly from insurance and liability-matching buyers—spreads remain near cycle tights. But beneath the surface, the market continues to offer pockets of opportunity. Companies with improving balance sheets, deleveraging stories, and idiosyncratic catalysts can still deliver attractive relative value, even if the overall index struggles to re-rate tighter.

Investors are also increasingly focused on potential "canaries in the coal mine," including Business Development Companies (BDCs) and technology, that may signal broader market stress to come (Exhibit 1). For BDCs, in our view, any signs of rising credit stress would have meaningful implications for the broader corporate landscape. Meanwhile, the tech sector, where mega-cap names have been pillars of index stability, faces growing scrutiny around regulation, capital expenditure (capex) intensity, and the sustainability of current leverage and growth assumptions. We believe weakness in either corner could be an early warning sign for IG credit more broadly.

High Yield: Risk-Off Trends Subside

By Bill Zox, CFA

High yield continues the positive run since late 2022, supported by the strength of equities on one side and core fixed income on the other. For the last two and a half years, the spread has been in a range of 260 to 460 basis points, based on the ICE BofA US High Yield Index. Defaults have been less than half the historic average, and recoveries have been high (Exhibit 1).

Absent a sustained bear market in equities or core fixed income, we expect the range to hold. Net new supply is starting to pick up (Exhibit 2). Spreads closer to the middle or high end of the range may be required for the demand.

Tariff-induced volatility caused some investors to hug the benchmark index and/or pile into BB-rated corporate credit, in our view, even at stretched valuations. This trend has led to the outperformance of BBs over Bs. Additionally, Bs have materially outperformed CCCs. Lastly, the top 100 high yield issuers have outperformed the broader market (Exhibit 3). These forces have reversed somewhat from the peak of the tariff-induced volatility, and we expect that to continue.

Emerging Markets: Improving Policy and Disinflation Outlook

By Michael Arno, CFA

For emerging markets (EM), 2025 was a strong year, despite persistent headwinds from evolving trade policy, multiple regional conflicts, and continued US-China tension. As we look ahead to 2026, the central question becomes where the next set of opportunities will emerge in the asset class. Hard currency sovereign and corporate spreads remain tight, limiting the potential for capital gains, yet carry still offers attractive income across segments of the universe (Exhibit 1). At the same time, the US dollar remains at historically elevated levels (Exhibit 2), foreigners remain heavily exposed to US assets (Exhibit 3), and the Federal Reserve is expected to continue rate cuts, taking some of the luster off the relative carry of the greenback. This setup should support another positive year from local currency markets, in our view. In particular, those markets seeing improving policy credibility and disinflation momentum stand out as increasingly compelling opportunities for both income and currency appreciation.

Policy uncertainty and market volatility defined much of 2025. The Trump administration’s tariff announcements triggered a broad risk-off move in April, followed by several rounds of retaliatory measures from China. Looking forward, 2026 is likely to bring a modest reduction in US policy risk. US and Chinese officials have reopened substantive dialogue, and Presidents Trump and Xi are scheduled for four meetings in 2026, including bilateral visits in each country. The appointment of a new Federal Reserve governor on May 15 could also shift the tone toward a more consensus-oriented Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), in contrast to the divisions seen at recent meetings. Markets continue to price a terminal rate near 3%. On the geopolitical front, momentum behind a Ukraine–Russia peace agreement raises the prospect of an end to the four-year conflict. In the US, Trump’s focus will increasingly turn to the 2026 midterms; with polls trending down, cost-of-living concerns have already led to tariff rollbacks on food and discussions around direct payments to households earning under $100,000. While policy unpredictability was a hallmark of 2025, we may now be past its peak as political constraints tighten ahead of the midterm cycle.

Within EM, local currency fixed income continues to offer selective appreciation potential against the US dollar. Opportunities remain particularly compelling in parts of the high yield universe, notably across Latin America, which faces a pivotal election calendar in 2026. Chile sees an early start with a December 2025 presidential runoff in which right-leaning candidate José Antonio Kast is widely expected to prevail. Although US policymakers have been active elsewhere in the region, Chile has received comparatively limited attention; a Kast victory could reopen discussions around minerals investment and processing partnerships. More broadly, the region appears to be shifting rightward: Peru’s early polls are led by Lima mayor Rafael López Aliaga; in Colombia, outsider candidate Abelardo—who has drawn inspiration from Milei and Bukele—is gaining traction; and in Brazil, it remains to be seen whether São Paulo Governor Tarcísio or a member of the Bolsonaro family will lead the opposition. A move toward the center-right across several key economies could prove a meaningful catalyst for rates and currency performance in the region. While strong returns prevailed across the region in 2025, yields remain quite elevated relative to the rest of local EM (Exhibit 4), setting up for another year of potentially solid returns.

China will be a key swing factor in 2026. With persistent weakness in the property sector, the main question is whether the export channel can continue to shoulder the burden of growth. Recent analysis, including work by Brad Setser at the Council on Foreign Relations, highlights that Chinese exports have risen roughly 40% since the pandemic, while imports have been essentially flat. As a result, China’s global trade surplus has continued to widen, raising the question of how long major trading partners will tolerate this trajectory. The broader challenge is whether China can successfully pivot toward a more balanced, domestically driven growth model and lift household consumption as a share of gross domestic product (GDP) back toward its historical highs. A move in that direction potentially will ease China’s deflation, pivoting Asian local currency bond yields higher as central banks’ easing cycles transition to a pause in 2026. The strength of the yuan (CNH) will ultimately be pivotal for low-yielding Asian local currencies. In that respect, it is conceivable that CNH will be on a strengthening path given China’s current account surplus and US dollar conversion from corporates. Under that scenario, selected undervalued Asian currencies could outperform.

Securitized Products: Constructive Outlook Continues

By Tracy Chen, CFA, CAIA

Over the quarter, not much changed in the outlook for securitized credit, which remains supported by anticipated monetary easing, solid household balance sheets, peak housing unaffordability, early signs of office real estate stabilization, supportive financial conditions, and favorable market technicals. Both fixed-rate sectors, such as BBB commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), jumbo prime residential MBS, and agency MBS, and floating-rate sectors, including credit risk transfers (CRTs) and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), have outperformed (Exhibit 1).

Looking ahead to 2026, the backdrop remains constructive. Resilient credit fundamentals, elevated all-in yields, relatively cheap valuations, fading rate volatility, and prospective easing of bank regulations should drive further spread tightening (Exhibit 2).

Key Opportunities:

  1. CRTs and RMBS: CRTs offer compelling all-in yields, rapid deleveraging, and potential upside from government-sponsored enterprise (GSE) tenders and reinvestment flows. RMBS performance remains supported by low unemployment and substantial accumulated home equity. We also see opportunities in RMBS, including non-qualified mortgage AAAs, prime jumbo AAA floaters, and CES/HELOC (Close-end second/Home equity line of credit), which offer scalability due to its rapid growth in new issuance.
  2. CLOs: Credit fundamentals of leveraged loans remain benign with CLO arbitrage economics improving. BBB and BB mezzanine tranches continue to deliver attractive yields with strong credit resilience. Reset/refinancing activity and sustained CLO ETF inflows are reinforcing liquidity and demand. Commercial real estate CLO AAAs also offer great opportunity for potential spread pickup.
  3. Agency MBS: These screen cheap versus investment grade corporate credit. The sector is insulated from potential corporate spread widening amid heavy AI-related issuance while incremental demand from GSEs, banks, REITs, and foreign investors provides a positive tailwind for further spread compression.
  4. CMBS: Tighter underwriting standards, distressed entry valuations, gradual stabilization in the office segment, limited new construction, and high sensitivity to lower rates should support spread tightening, particularly in seasoned conduit and Single-Asset Single-Borrower (SASB) exposures.
  5. ABS: Data-center and fiber ABS provide access to the strong fundamentals supporting AI-related infrastructure. These deals offer shorter duration, structural protections, IG ratings, scalability, and attractive relative value.

Across the securitized universe, subdued net issuance further strengthens technicals. Taken together, these factors create a compelling opportunity set in structured credit heading into 2026.

Global Currencies: Elevated Dollar Remains Vulnerable

By Anujeet Sareen, CFA

After a sharp decline in early 2025, the US dollar has drifted higher over the past few months. Negative US policy shocks have subsided, particularly with some of the growth offsets in the reconciliation bill passed earlier this year. Meanwhile, investor enthusiasm around growth opportunities in artificial intelligence (AI) has remained firm, attracting global capital flows into US equities.

Yet at the same time, US interest rates have declined in absolute and relative terms. While US growth bounced in the third quarter, the US labor market visibly softened, catalyzing the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates further. Meanwhile, business surveys outside the US continue to strengthen, notably in Europe. The combination of monetary easing and fiscal easing outside the US supports further improvement in economic activity in the rest of the world through the balance of 2026. As growth differentials narrow between the US and the rest of the world, the dollar is likely to weaken further.

From a longer-term valuation perspective, the dollar is still very elevated in broad trade-weighted terms. It therefore remains vulnerable to a further shift in investor expectations on relative macro fundamentals (Exhibit 1).

It is worth noting that the dollar’s performance since mid-year has not been uniform. The dollar continued to weaken against the Chinese renminbi and emerging market currencies, notably higher-yielding currencies such as the Brazilian real. Over that same period, the dollar rallied against developed market currencies, particularly the Japanese yen. Ultimately, we expect the dollar to decline on a broad basis into the end of 2026.

In the near term, the US economy is set to benefit from further fiscal stimulus in the first quarter. It is possible that a rise in US yields, perhaps catalyzed by a more hawkish Federal Reserve, temporarily lifts the dollar over the first few months of 2026. However, we think any such rally in the dollar is unlikely to last, as the fiscal support will not extend beyond the first quarter. Meanwhile, the rest of the world will show progress toward stronger growth throughout the year.

Global Equities: The Biases We Bring, the Opportunities They Create

By Sorin Roibu, CFA

On the surface, investing appears simple, but that is precisely why it is profoundly difficult. Human beings were built for survival, not for outperforming global equity benchmarks. We come preloaded with cognitive shortcuts that can work against us: groupthink, social comparison, fear of missing out, loss aversion, overconfidence, and emotional decision-making. Every investor begins at a behavioral deficit, and acknowledging these limitations is the first step in overcoming them.

History provides endless reminders that the largest investment mistakes occur when people become convinced they can forecast the future with precision. Forecasting is the Achilles heel of investing. Most predictions fail, usually at the exact moment consensus becomes strongest. Whether it was the belief that COVID would suppress global activity for years or today’s conviction that artificial intelligence will reshape society in a straight line, expectations tend to overshoot reality far more often than they match it. Markets do not reward certainty; they reward mispriced expectations.

The current market environment offers a vivid example. In 2024, the US grew to nearly two-thirds of global equity market capitalization, a level not seen in decades. Valuations in large US technology companies embedded perfection while investors extrapolated far into the future both the AI revolution and the idea of US exceptionalism. At the same time, a broad set of global equities in Europe, Japan, Brazil, Indonesia, and even parts of China traded at low expectations despite improving fundamentals and identifiable catalysts.

The early part of 2025 showed how quickly expectations can shift (Exhibit 1). Non-US markets began to outperform, helped by fiscal stimulus, monetary easing outside the US, more attractive valuations, and industry-specific supply and demand imbalances. Commercial aviation shortages, energy underinvestment, and structural bottlenecks are creating opportunities in places that investors have largely ignored.

These imbalances are why our portfolio is positioned toward the unloved, the underowned, and the underpriced. The most compelling opportunities are often where expectations are low and fundamentals have room to surprise on the upside. Today that describes many companies outside the US (Exhibit 2).

As we look ahead to 2026, we see the opportunity set broadening, particularly in regions and sectors where expectations remain muted. Our portfolio continues to carry a significant underweight to the US and meaningful overweights in Europe, the UK, and parts of emerging markets. Many of these markets combine low valuations, improving earnings trends, and supportive policy dynamics.

At the same time, we remain selective around AI. Revolutionary technologies can be life-changing and bubble-prone at the same time. Railway mania in the 1840s, the dot-com period, and today’s data center buildout all share the same pattern. Our focus is on the broader ecosystem of beneficiaries rather than the narrow group of headline winners.

The lesson for investors is simple. Manage your biases, question consensus, focus on valuations, and think in probabilities. Markets are complex, adaptive systems that constantly shift. The best opportunities often appear where the crowd is least willing to look.

US Equities: Market Performance May Broaden

By Patrick Kaser, CFA

We are heading into 2026 after a year with starts and stops on momentum and artificial intelligence stocks as a leading driver of performance. In many ways the setup ahead resembles a year ago: a market with concentrated positions at the top and hopes of AI investment generating future profits. At the same time, however, the risks feel larger as major technology companies face outsize expectations. For example, OpenAI is surrounded by circular financing and frequent press around competitive challenges, and its future success also may drive the outlook for companies ranging from Oracle to NVIDIA.

While politics seems to have dominated 2025, from tariffs to wars, the stock market has mostly shrugged off that noise. However, the year leading up to the midterms is often a year of underperformance, and we believe that political puts and takes will be a larger driver of returns in 2026. With so many political issues on the table, it is hard to point to just one that would dominate, but our view that Democrats are likely to retake the House plus the President’s declining popularity, advanced age, and likely waning influence as he heads toward lame-duck status may cause turbulence if the White House aims to force policies through while it has the chance. This push could provide additional fiscal stimulus, although we also believe businesses may not view policy changes as permanent.

We believe that economic momentum is likely to pick up somewhat, which, combined with interest rate cuts, is more likely to cause market performance to broaden out relative to the past few years. This shift should, generally, be good for small- and mid-cap companies and value stocks on a relative basis. The underperformance of low-beta stocks, which now tend to be more in the value universe, may also reverse as investors rotate their exposures.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Here Group: Decoding Post-Restructuring Valuation Gap
data.description:
Almost three months after QuantaSing rebranded as Here Group (HERE), the stock fell out of the $10s and now sits miles away from the $11 price target I had before. Wall St. isn’t far off either—most folks there still have this sitting at a ‘Strong Buy’ with targets well into the $10s.

Are we all wrong? I don't think so. Not in the big picture, at least.

But we all definitely overshot what 'Silver Economy' businesses can actually sell for. And of course, sitting on less cash, the business would be worth less.

That's why I would like to reach another price target at the end of this write-up.

But once you check the restructuring details, you see that Here Group sold everything else for around $25.5 million (RMB 162 million plus $2.5 million).

As part of the proposed business restructuring and in connection with the termination of the VIE Agreements, the company entered into share transfer agreements with the relevant entities of a third-party buyer, pursuant to which Beijing Liangzizhige will transfer all of its equity interests in Beijing Feierlai and Beijing Chuangyuqizhi to the buyer, and the company will transfer all of its equity interests in QS International and Rare River to the buyer, for an aggregate consideration of RMB162 million and US$2.5 million.

Considering I was already expecting dirt-cheap multiples—something like 0.4x sales for the EdTech side (basically 97% of FY 2025 revenue), 1x for wellness + baijiu, and about 0.6x for Enterprise Services (which barely moves the needle anyway), we missed by a lot.

To give you an idea, this $25.5 million represents 0.06x FY 2025 sales.

Something very low, even if we consider multiples of pure Chinese EdTech such as Gaotu Techedu (GOTU) (0.4x), Jinxin Technology (NAMI) (0.6x), and New Oriental Education (EDU) (0.9x).

I mean, a bunch of those segments were already falling apart revenue-wise, but even with that in mind, the price was still surprisingly low.

Is that the whole story? I doubt it.

Digging a little deeper into their balance sheet (it's difficult to know beforehand what each brand's liabilities were), you can see that after the restructuring, they removed $54.6 million in liabilities.

That is, if we look at it this way, the transaction reached ~$80 million (a multiple of 0.2x sales), still $70 million less than my previous estimates, but now it's starting to be justified.

Most of the liabilities (just over $30 million) came from unearned revenues tied to QuantaSing's subscription business, and ultimately the balance sheet ended up much cleaner than before.

Just take a look at this chart:

This is also part of the transaction, isn't it?

And if you look at the latest balance sheet, assets now outweigh liabilities by roughly 4.3x—way above the 2.2x we saw at the end of FY 2025, basically just last quarter.

Q1 FY 2026: First Quarter Pure-Pop Toys

But before we tweak the price target considering the actual restructuring, we've got to talk about Here Group's first quarter as a pure pop-toy company.

Starting with sales, Here pulled in $17.9 million in revenue in Q1 FY 2026, basically a 93.3% jump from the previous quarter (that's the only real comparison we've got for now).

What I found interesting was that in the earnings call they gave exactly how much each IP contributed to sales.

WAKUKU—their cheaper Labubu look-alike and the clear flagship—carried the quarter with 71% of sales ($12.7 million). The other two largest chunks were divided between ZIYULI (16% or $2.8 million) and SIINONO (10% or $1.7 million). Obviously, everything runs through WAKUKU. How well it executes pretty much shapes the whole top line.

Looking at the margins now, Here managed to pull off a gross margin of 41.2%, significantly higher than the 34.7% they delivered in the last quarter.

Of course, if we compare this to their previous business model, you'll see that it's completely different. Don't compare pears to apples. As they scale it, costs dilute, the model tightens up, and fixed costs get eaten by the revenue ramp.

So, it is expected that this will actually happen.

But what if we compare it to Pop Mart (PMRTY)? Put this way, Here still has a long climb ahead, especially since the competitor is pulling off a hefty ~70% gross margin thanks to scale and pricing power.

In any case, Here is already running higher gross margins than Funko (FNKO). And with sales growing this fast, it’s not a stretch to imagine those margins climbing to +50% in the years ahead.

Looking at the rest, all the gross profit generated ($7.4 million) was eaten up by SG&A and R&D, causing them to report an EBITDA of -$3.8 million. On the bottom line, the diluted and normalized EPS was -$0.04.

This is nothing unusual for businesses that are scaling, and, judging by the current trend, it is likely that they will start reporting positive EPS in just under a year.

Here also ended with $110.7 million in net cash on its balance sheet, allowing them to weather cash shortfalls along the way.

Since I don’t think they’ll need much of that cash to keep things running, I wouldn’t be surprised if they start buying back shares at these levels. They did approve a $20 million repurchase plan earlier this year, and with $17.8 million still unused, this would be a pretty good moment to put it to work.

If they put the entire amount to work at around $6 to $7 a share, that would take roughly 2.5 to 3 million shares off the table.

Basically a mid-single-digit shareholder yield for just over 15% of cash. Not bad, don't you think?

These are just conjectures at the end of the day.

Stores Start Popping Up

The CEO made it pretty clear that Here plans to grow a little differently at home than abroad. Outside of China, the strategy is basically all about partnering with retailers like Miniso (MNSO), while in China they'll keep a handful of flagship stores.

In the last quarter, they opened three pop-up stores (Shanghai, Beijing, and Shenzhen). Together, they generated just under $0.5 million this quarter (just under 3% of total sales).

It looks like a tiny footprint at first glance. And it is. But chances are this pile is going to grow.

In fact, they are planning at least four large stores in high-traffic areas like Shenzhen and Shanghai, and these will be larger and permanent—unlike the pop-ups they opened before.

Here are a few pictures I found of the pop-ups and the stores that are already up and open:

The bulk of these stores should open next year and will help boost sales. I don't think these shops will move the needle beyond a small single-digit chunk of sales, but they should have an impact on brand buzz.

The Billion Dollar Question: What Is the Price Target?

Looking down the road, nothing has changed in their guidance:

  • For Q2 FY 2026: Revenue between $21.2 and $22.6 million (+18.4 to 26.2% compared to Q1 FY 2026). Note that they are assuming that pop-toys sales will slow down a bit, which makes me think they are being too conservative.
  • For the entire FY 2026: Revenues between $106.1 million and $113.2 million.

Another thing we need to factor in is the net cash position, which sits at $110.7 million after the restructuring and the asset sales.

In this setup, with an EV of $211.8 million, the stock is trading at roughly 1.9x sales.

Looking ahead to FY 2026, I'd expect Here to trade below Pop Mart (no surprise there, right?) but still above the more established names that sit around 1 to 2x sales. With Pop Mart at 5.8x, a 2 to 3x range feels like a fair baseline.

At the midpoint—both for revenue and the multiple—that would put the stock somewhere near $7 a share.

However, if we look at FY 2027, Wall St. projects a consensus revenue of $305 million!

That's right, you didn't misread, that's close to triple Here's own FY 2026 target.

Taking this projection and stipulating the same midpoint multiple of 2.5x sales, we would arrive at a price target of ~$16 per share. Discounting to present value from a Cost of Equity of approximately 9%, we could tweak it to $13.50.

This tells me that Wall Street’s expectations are sky-high, while the market itself seems to be underestimating the company’s ability to sustain anything close to this near triple-digit growth rate.

How about a middle ground?

Well, as much as I believe sales will continue to rise rapidly for FY 2027, I don't believe they will reach close to $300 million. Perhaps $200 million (up ~80% year-over-year from the target) is more realistic here.

That said, applying the same assumptions in this model, we would arrive at a price target close to $9.50 per share.

Putting it all together, I land at a price target of roughly $8 per share. That already incorporates the restructuring data and assumes a fair 2.5x sales multiple, along with the idea that they can keep growing revenue at double-digit rates quarter after quarter throughout FY 2026.

Now you can see that the post-restructuring stock slump makes sense. But, as you can see, I think this has all gone too far.

At $5.15, this offers us an upside of +50% at these levels, and that's without even considering buybacks.

As I told you in the last write-up, Here Group is highly speculative, but it offers exposure to the pop-toy segment at relatively low multiples and a clean balance sheet.

For me, it remains a long-term 'Buy'. And of course, don't risk a large part of your portfolio on this horse (I mean, WAKUKU!). High risk, high reward.

data.title: Duke Energy: I Am Buying The Recent Dip
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Within the context of an increasingly unpredictable market, I am looking to gradually increase my position in defensive stocks with a decent dividend. My strategy is to look for relatively safe investments. I intend to further reduce risk by investing in multiple companies represented in various industries. Duke Energy Corporation (DUK) fits the profile within the utilities segment of the market. It is showing steady returns, a safe, sustainable dividend, and at the same time, there is some growth potential. I decided to buy the recent dip, and I intend to add incrementally if its share price declines from here.

Duke Energy Company Profile & Latest Results

Duke Energy operates mostly in the Southeastern part of the US, from Florida to Indiana. It operates various power plants, including nuclear, solar, hydro, and thermal. It also provides natural gas to 1.7 million households and businesses, in addition to providing power to about 8.6 million customers for electric power.

As the chart shows, its stock price is relatively stable, with a roughly 26% gain for the past five years. The dividend that currently yields 3.7% helps to shore up total long-term returns.

The latest financial results and forecasts suggest that Duke Energy will continue performing steadily. Revenues came in at $24.3 billion for the first nine months of the year, up just over 5% compared with the same period from a year earlier. Net income came in at $3.87 billion in the first nine months of the year, up about 14% compared with the same period from a year earlier.

One of the more concerning aspects of the company's financial profile is its rising debt-financing cost. In the first nine months of the year, those costs came in at $2.69 billion, which constitutes about 11% of revenues. It is far above my 5% threshold, where one has to start worrying about the amount of a company's revenues that goes toward servicing the debt. I find such high levels to be worrying because they eat away at self-sustaining resources that a company can employ for capital investments without relying solely on debt.

How Duke Energy Compares To Peers

As far as Duke Energy's past performance and future expectations go versus industry peers such as NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE), America's largest utility company, Duke Energy's shares have been outperforming.

Duke has a more generous dividend, which helps to bolster total returns per share. Its forward P/E ratio is lower at 18.5 compared with NextEra's at nearly 22. On this basis, we can conclude that the outperformance of Duke Energy's shares compared with its industry peer was based on fundamentals.

I want to note the fact that NextEra is similarly to Duke Energy saddled with high interest costs. In the third quarter of this year, its interest-to-revenue ratio was about 14%. The high interest burden can be attributed to the fact that capital investments can be very high in this industry, with long wait times for a return on the investments.

Industry & Company-Specific Positive Factors

In addition to company-specific metrics and factors, for a company to have investment potential, it must have accompanying external factors working in its favor. In the case of Duke Energy, I see a number of potential external factors that have the potential to boost its revenue and profit growth.

Duke Energy arguably has a geographical advantage over many peers, since it operates in many of the fastest-growing states in terms of demographics as well as business investment.

Most of the states where Duke Energy operates are well known for robust demographic and business-sector growth. Logic dictates that, with population growth & development, Duke Energy stands to benefit from it as well. It may not be anything nearly as exciting as the expected growth in AI-related tech or other market segments, but it promises to be steady, and as of right now, it looks like nearly a sure thing.

Utilities may also potentially benefit from the AI trend, depending on how data centers are set to be provided with the massive energy requirements that are forecast to be necessary to run them. Unlike the growth in population and business activities specific to the areas where Duke Energy operates, the AI factor is nowhere near as certain & predictable. There are some doubts about the magnitude of AI development regarding issues such as monetization. The net effect on utilities may not be as great if, as is often reported, many of these data centers will be powered by energy sources that will be built in conjunction with the data centers by the companies involved in building them. Modular nuclear reactors seem to be a favored option.

A company-specific factor that, in my view, protects Duke Energy's profitability prospects is its nuclear power portfolio. With its six nuclear power plants in the Carolinas, it can partially limit the impact of uncertainties such as coal or natural gas price fluctuations and renewable energy, given the tendency to have steady power generation costs once the sizable initial capital investment in building the nuclear power plants has been completed. Unlike coal-fired plants or natural gas, the fuel used in nuclear power plants comprises a small percentage of the total lifetime cost of building and operating a nuclear power plant; therefore, uranium price fluctuations do not have a massive impact on the cost of nuclear energy.

Negative Factors & Risks

My number one concern with Duke Energy and its prospects is the interest rate risk factor, given its already high debt and debt-servicing costs, which make this a high-interest-rate-risk company. Currently, Fed rate cuts dominate the headlines, so interest rate risks do not seem like an imminent issue. As I pointed out in a recent article, risks of higher interest rates, especially if my view that starting as early as the second half of 2026, we will start to see a sustained commodities price rally take hold, which is inflationary, may be more elevated than the market currently expects.

A potential sustained rally in commodities prices is also a risk for Duke Energy. It has exposure to natural gas, coal, and even copper prices. My personal view is that we are getting very close to the US shale revolution going into reverse, even as Europe is now getting about a quarter of its natural gas imports from the US. I believe that US natural gas prices are on a sustained upward trajectory, which will help to squeeze more gas out of increasingly mature shale fields and keep domestic demand growth in check, which will help to sustain continued exports of LNG to Europe as well. For utilities, this means higher input prices for generating electricity, some of which they can pass on to consumers, while some of it they will not be able to.

Conclusions

I decided to initiate a small position in Duke Energy shares on a significant recent pullback of almost 10% from its all-time highs. I intend to add incrementally from here, if its share price declines further, as long as my original buy thesis holds up. This is a long-term investment bet, based on the company-specific as well as the external fundamentals I cited.

My investment in Duke Energy shares is a part of a broader strategy I adopted recently of seeking defensive stocks to add to my portfolio in expectation of possible market risks starting next year. I also started buying Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB) shares early in the fall with the same broader safety play in mind. My strategy is to identify multiple stocks in the next few months for this purpose in order to reduce industry or company-specific risks. I intend to keep my exposure to these stocks at less than 3% of my total stock portfolio.

North Carolina legislators were recently cited to oppose Duke Energy's proposed increase of 15% in its rates for residential homes by 2028. It is a highlight of the difficulties that the company will face in attempts to capitalize on growing demand as well as defend its profit margins in the face of rising capital & operating costs. Despite such challenges, there are prospects for growth and even improving profitability that can drive this stock steadily higher, which, combined with its dividend, has the potential to provide for a solid long-term return on investment. At the moment, I see further short-term weakness in Duke Energy's share price as an opportunity to add to my position.

data.title: Capital Demands Soar For New Copper Supply
data.description:
The accelerating global energy transition has enhanced the demand for copper, yet bringing new supply online has become increasingly costly. A key metric for measuring this financial challenge is capital intensity, which for this analysis is defined as the total development capital expenditure divided by a mine's average annual copper production. Our findings show a clear trade-off between upfront capital and long-term operating costs and a more expensive future for copper supply.

The take

➤ The weighted-average capital intensity for 26 upcoming copper projects, scheduled to start by 2030, is $22,359 per metric ton of annual paid copper. This marks a substantial increase in the upfront investment required to bring new production online, signaling a more expensive future for copper supply.

➤ The US and Canada region hosts the most capital-intensive projects, with the Copper World, Gunnison (GCUMF) and Casino projects driving the regional average up.

➤ Large projects, such as Reko Diq, can achieve near-average capital intensity due to their massive production scale. This contrasts with smaller mines like Copperwood that have low initial capex but may face higher long-term operating costs.

Our analysis of 26 primary copper projects, with target start date within five years, have an average capital intensity of $22,359 per metric ton of paid copper per year. The development capex for this pipeline ranges from $172 million to $9.14 billion, marking a substantial increase from our previous study, which recorded a lower capex range between $53 million and $5.45 billion.

Out of the 22 projects previously analyzed, 13 have postponed their target start dates but are still included in our latest review. These delays have resulted in adjustments in development costs. A notable project is Capstone Copper Corp.'s (CSCCF) Santo Domingo in Chile, which recorded the largest increase of $910 million, representing a 55% jump from the budget reported in the 2020 technical report. This escalation is primarily driven by the addition of a mine fleet, enhanced support infrastructure and expanded copper and magnetite circuits.

These changes are designed to process an additional 43.8 million metric tons of life-of-mine ore, boosting annual copper production to 68,500 metric tons from 62,000 metric tons and gold output to 22,000 ounces from 17,000 ounces. With the revised production profile and capex, Santo Domingo's capital intensity for copper now stands at $37,553/t/y, which is among the highest in our list.

Similar to other capital-intensive copper projects, Santo Domingo requires more complex processing. A common theme for low-capital-intensity projects is recovering a single product, allowing for a more straightforward approach. For instance, Ero Copper Corp.'s (ERO) Tucuma project in Brazil ranks as the second-least capital-intensive and utilizes the conventional flotation method to recover copper concentrate.

Beyond processing methods, Tucuma's open-pit mining approach and favorable location have also contributed to its lower initial capital requirements. Regionally, majority of upcoming projects are concentrated in Asia-Pacific, with 10 assets, followed by eight in Latin America, seven in the US and Canada, and just one in Africa. Latin America has the lowest average capital intensity for copper, with values ranging from $10,566/t/y to $37,553/t/y, resulting in an average of $19,049/t/y. In contrast, the US and Canada region has the highest average, at $28,910/t/y. Development capex in this region spans from $421 million to $2.72 billion, with annual copper production ranging from 27,780 metric tons to 91,110 metric tons.

Three major copper projects in the US and Canada — Copper World, Gunnison, and Casino — illustrate the high capital intensity associated with large-scale, technically complex mines. These costs are driven by factors such as remote locations, stringent environmental regulations and advanced processing requirements. Hudbay Minerals Inc.'s (HBM) Copper World project in Arizona deploys a two-phase development process over 20 years. It has an initial capex of $1.32 billion and a subsequent $367 million expansion for solvent extraction and electrowinning (SX-EW) and leaching systems, resulting in a capital intensity of $40,137/t/y. Gunnison, also in Arizona, and Casino in Yukon exhibit nearly identical capital intensities of approximately $30,000/t/y. Gunnison incurs additional costs for relocating a 2.8-mile section of Interstate 10, and Casino requires extensive infrastructure — including an airstrip, a 132-km main access gravel road, a processing plant and a campsite — and preproduction operations ahead of its anticipated 2030 commercial start. Despite Copper World's high upfront cost, its estimated all-in sustaining cost (AISC) of $2,936/t is substantially lower than Gunnison's $4,884/t. NorthMet in Minnesota, the most capital-intensive mine in the study, has been excluded from further analysis because its primary commodity can be nickel, depending on the price environment.

Another significant copper project to come online before 2030 is Barrick Mining Corp.'s (B) Reko Diq in Pakistan, which hosts approximately 15 MMt of copper reserves — the largest among the projects in our analysis. Commercial production is scheduled to begin in 2028, with an anticipated annual output of 377,000 metric tons of copper over a 40-year mine life. This immense anticipated production at Reko Diq resulted in a $24,240/t/y capital intensity tracking close to the global average, despite having the highest development capex in the study of around $9.14 billion. Furthermore, the mine's AISC is estimated to average $4,047/t over the life of the mine, positioning it comfortably below the study average of $5,565/t. In contrast, Highland Copper Co. Inc.'s (HDRSF) Copperwood project in Michigan, US, leverages existing road networks and power substations, resulting in one of the lowest capital intensities at $14,708/t/y. However, the mine faces elevated operating costs, with an AISC of $6,014/t, placing it in the upper half of the copper cost curve. These higher costs primarily reflect the planned use of the room-and-pillar mining method for ore extraction, combined with the higher power rate associated with constructing and operating transmission lines and a substation.

The Reko Diq copper project is comparable in scale to the Kamoa-Kakula operation in the Democratic Republic of Congo, which holds 17.7 MMt of copper reserves and began commercial production in 2021. Kamoa-Kakula's capital intensity of $10,177/t/y ranks among the lowest when compared with the current project pipeline, while its AISC averaged $5,054/t from 2021 through 2024 — within the lower half of the global cost curve. The AISC is expected to rise, however, with the 2030–35 forecast average to be 24% higher than the 2021–24 average.

Several structural factors are making copper mines around the world increasingly capital-intensive and operationally expensive, despite the rising energy transition demands. These include persistent inflation in equipment and labor costs, the need to develop more technically complex processing methods to treat lower-grade ore bodies, and stricter environmental and social permitting requirements. Furthermore, recent macroeconomic developments — including new tariffs on materials — are expected to add further cost pressures. Ultimately, the market will need to support a higher sustained copper price to incentivize the massive investment required to meet future demand and avoid a critical supply bottleneck in the future.
